exemplar.title: Investigating the Anchoring Bias and the influence on decision-making (replication of Stack and Mussweiler (1977)

detailedExemplarData.title: Investigating the Anchoring Bias and the influence on decision-making (replication of Stack and Mussweiler (1977)

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Aim clearly stated and its relevance to anchoring bias and the original study is well explained.
Theory of anchoring bias accurately defined and linked to the planned replication.
Hypotheses are explicitly stated with independent and dependent variables operationalised.
weaknesses: Minor misattribution of heuristics as âassumptionsâ rather than mental shortcuts.
Incomplete citation details for the secondary source on biases in thinking.
Small inaccuracies in historical attribution (Tversky & Kahneman vs. Tversky & Mussweiler).
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The introduction consistently meets the top band descriptors for aim relevance, theory connection and operationalised hypothesis. Minor inaccuracies and citation omissions prevent a perfect mark, so a score of 5/6 reflects strong but not flawless performance.

strengths: Research design is explained, with justification for using an independent-measures approach and random allocation.
Sampling technique and participant characteristics are described and the reasoning for choosing this sample is explained.
Controlled variables and choice of materials are not only listed but each is justified in terms of validity and bias reduction.
weaknesses: One procedural step is incompletely described, leaving ambiguity.
Limitations of opportunity sampling (e.g., generalisability, selection bias) are not addressed.
Additional controls (e.g., experimenter training, calibration of recording apps) could be included for greater rigor.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three exploration strands are clearly explained and justified, meeting the highest band. Minor procedural and sampling-limitations gaps are present, hence a full 4/4.

strengths: Appropriate use of descriptive (means, SD) and inferential (MannâWhitney U) statistics.
Graphical presentation directly addresses the hypothesis with correct chart choice and error bars.
Findings are linked to the hypothesis with clear acceptance of the null and reference to descriptive trends.
weaknesses: Missing sample size (n) in tables and graphs, and incomplete axis labels.
Absence of effect size reporting and exact p-value undermines inferential clarity.
Formatting flaws in the graph (small font, pixelation) reduce readability.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While the statistical and graphical methods are appropriate, multiple technical omissions (n values, effect size, exact p) and presentation flaws limit the work to a mid-lower band. A score of 2/6 reflects competent but incomplete analysis.

strengths: Limitations are identified, explained and directly linked to validity or generalisability.
Modifications are explicitly tied to specific limitations and justified.
Discussion of findings references background theory and related literature on anchoring plausibility.
weaknesses: Deeper links between strengths/limitations and validity types (internal, external) are missing.
No quantification of confounds (e.g., prior knowledge) to support evaluation.
Misstatement regarding p=0.05 threshold; exact p-value should be provided for accuracy.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation thoroughly meets top-band descriptors with justified modifications and theory-driven discussion. However, suggestions for deeper validity analysis and a minor statistical inaccuracy reduce the score to 3/6.

strandSelections.reasoning: A bar chart with error bars (Â±1 SD) clearly compares the two conditions and is directly linked to the hypothesis. Axes are labelled and the graphic is interpreted in the text. Minor formatting flaws (pixelated image, small font) prevent full marks but the presentation is technically correct and purposeful.

The student states the inferential outcome (no significant difference), explicitly accepts the null hypothesis, and reconciles this with descriptive trends. The interpretation is clearly tied back to the hypothesis and data, fulfilling the top-band descriptor though with limited depth.

Both hypotheses are stated; the IV (low vs high anchor) and DV (numerical age estimate) are not only identified but operationalised in concrete terms. Controls are mentioned, and wording makes it explicit how each condition differs. Because operationalisation is precise and unambiguous, the strand meets the very top of the highest band.

Opportunity sampling is defined, participant characteristics (age, gender balance, SES, language ability) are listed, and the reason for choosing non-psychology sixth-formers is provided. These comments explain, not just list, the sampling decisions, satisfying the higher band.

Anchoring bias is accurately defined, its origin in Tversky & Kahneman (1974) is noted, and Strack & Mussweiler (1997) is summarised. The student then connects this model to the planned replication, showing how the bias should influence judgments of Churchillâs age. Minor inaccuracies (calling heuristics âassumptionsâ) prevent a flawless mark, but the description and link are clearly present and correct.

Findings are related to anchoring theory and to literature on plausibility (Wilson et al.). The discussion moves beyond simple restatement, considering why extreme anchors may have failed. Coverage is competent but not exhaustive, meriting a solid score in the highest band.

The student clearly states the purpose â to replicate Strack & Mussweiler (1997) and test the impact of extreme numerical anchors on estimates of Churchillâs age. The paragraph explicitly links this aim to the broader concept of anchoring bias and explains why the original study is relevant to the new context. While concise, the explanation makes the relevance evident without leaving major gaps, meriting a solid but not perfect score in the top band.

Several strengths (random allocation, avoidance of order effects) and limitations (noise, prior knowledge, sample size) are explained and explicitly linked to validity or generalisability. The points are clearly relevant to the investigation, matching the descriptor for the top band.

Controlled variables (quiet environment, identical wording, same consent procedure) and materials (consent forms, briefing/debriefing scripts, recording apps) are described and each control is briefly justified for validity or demand-characteristic reduction. This constitutes an explanation rather than a simple list.

Each limitation is followed by a concrete, justified modification (e.g., secluded room to reduce distractions; matched-pairs design to control prior knowledge; more plausible anchors). The links between problems and solutions are explicit, satisfying the requirements for the highest band.

The student names an independent-measures design, explains random allocation, and justifies the choice by referencing order effects. This moves beyond mere description into explanation, fitting the upper band for Exploration.

Mean, standard deviation and a MannâWhitney U are calculated. Normality concerns are cited as justification for the non-parametric test, and the decision criterion is applied correctly. Although the critical value table is reproduced rather than referenced, there are no obvious calculation errors, so the analysis is appropriate and largely accurate.

annotations.comment: The student equates heuristics to âassumptions,â which misrepresents their cognitive role as mental shortcuts. Clarify that heuristics are systematic mental strategies, not mere suppositions, to strengthen the theoretical description.
The citation âDP Psychology: Biases in Thinking and Decision-makingâ lacks full reference details. Provide author, year, and page or URL to meet academic conventions and support theory linkage.

The description of Strack and Mussweiler (1977) is accurate, but the link to how their extreme anchors parallel the Churchill replication could be made more explicit. Expand on the relevance to your aim.
The aim is clearly stated, but referencing Tversky and Mussweiller is inaccurateâthe original study was by Tversky & Kahneman. Correct the attribution to maintain historical accuracy.

The independent and dependent variables are correctly identified and operationalised, but describing the DV as âguess of Winston Churchillâs death in yearsâ could be refined to âestimated age at death in yearsâ for precision.
The hypothesis states a directional difference but omits the null or alternative phrasing convention. Consider framing both null and research hypotheses explicitly to meet formal standards.

Random allocation is justified to avoid order effects, but the rationale for not using matched pairs could be explored. Discuss potential trade-offs between designs to deepen research design evaluation.
Opportunity sampling is well described, but explain its limitations in terms of generalisability and potential selection bias, linking back to your sampleâs context.
The control of environment and wording reduces biases well, but consider adding experimenter training as a control to ensure consistent delivery across participants.
Materials include consent and recording apps appropriately. To strengthen validity, specify calibration steps for the recording app or observer consistency checks.
The procedural steps are clear, but step 3 is incomplete (âOnce researchers got a sufficient numberâ¦â). Ensure all steps are fully described to avoid procedural ambiguity.
The table correctly reports means and SDs, but the caption omits sample sizes (n). Include n for each condition to make the table self-contained.
Axes on the bar chart lack exact scale labels for units and sample size. Label axes clearly with âMean estimated age (years)â and note n to enhance clarity.
The narrative interprets overlapping error bars to infer non-significance. This is descriptive; include the U statistic and exact p-value in the figure caption or text for inferential support.
The MannâWhitney U result is stated without effect size. Include a measure such as rank-biserial correlation to quantify the magnitude of the (non-significant) effect.
Interpreting the U-value as ânot significant at 0.05â is correct, but specify whether p>0.05 or exact p to avoid ambiguity.
The discussion appropriately references Wilson et al. on plausibility, but could extend to explain how plausibility thresholds affect anchoring strength in real-world contexts.
Strengths such as random allocation are well identified. To deepen evaluation, link each strength to specific validity types (internal, external, ecological).
The limitation regarding prior knowledge is pertinent. Consider measuring participantsâ baseline history knowledge to quantify this confound rather than assuming its presence.
The matched-pairs modification is justified, but discuss how grouping by prior knowledge would be implemented and its impact on statistical power.
The discussion on anchor plausibility is strong. Further evaluation could propose an intermediate anchor range and predict its expected effectiveness using anchoring distance theory.
The conclusion restates that pâ=â0.05, but pâ=â0.05 is the threshold rather than observed result. Clarify whether p>0.05 and provide the exact p-value for accuracy.
